evidence_quotes.quote: publishers destroying video games that they have paid for
Ubisoft's EULA regarding that game was hidden and needlessly obscured and predatory for the consumers

paragraphs.text: Hi. I would like to request that the Digital Fairness Act adds protections to customers from publishers destroying video games that they have paid for. You guys have probably already heard of and received similar suggestions that mentions the Stop Killing Games' EU Citizens Initiative that also aims to stop this issue and has our support. But not every country in the EU can sign the initiative even if they are EU adjacent, like Norway and Iceland. So this is an alternative for us to voice our support and add some extra weight on top of this. I'm personally interested in stopping publishers from destroying video games too. Like many others, I'm also one who owned Ubisoft's The Crew before it got destroyed due to it always being an online only game that lacked an end of support plan that would ensure that it was still playable after support ends. And whatever was written in Ubisoft's EULA regarding that game was hidden and needlessly obscured and predatory for the consumers, which isn't right no matter how you look at it. Ubisoft are also one of the biggest publishers that want to destroy their games after ending support, and who are pushing against the whole Stop Killing Games movement. And they have more games other than The Crew, which also are at risk of getting destroyed in the future. The Crew 2 and The Crew Motorfest, the sequel games pose that risk. Though due to all the backlash of the first game getting destroyed, Ubisoft did backpedal somewhat and announced offline modes to be added to those two titles so that in the future, it would still be playable after end of support. But we haven't heard any more news since that announcement, so it's entirely possible that they could change their minds on that subject. Another Ubisoft title, Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon Breakpoint is also at risk of getting destroyed once support ends since that is also a video game that requires a constant connection to servers at Ubisoft in other to play the game. Even when playing just single-player. And we have not heard anything about that title getting and offline mode. Ubisoft tried to claim years ago that they couldn't do an offline mode to this game. But that is just them lying to us customers, because they don't want to put the extra effort into it. Sorry for making this long, it probably didn't need to be that long. But it was important to be thorough about our predicament and what we want out of this. We want protections to ensure that future games, and hopefully some already released games, have mandatory plans in place put on the publishers to ensure that once support ends for a game, it stays playable for the consumers and doesn't get destroyed in an instant. Like what happened with The Crew, and countless other games. And if we can't achieve that, then the publishers will absolutely continue with destroying video games after a certain amount of years if it's convenient for them. A planned obsolescence, but for video games of all things. That is just really messed up. Hope this gets addressed for the betterment of the consumers who paid and spent a lot of time in the video games. Best Regards Patrick Bull
